nativeRange.habitat: Sporophyte parent species inhabits humid Southeast Asian forests up to 1,200 m elevation, growing epiphytically on trees or among rocks in dry river beds; critically endangered in the wild in Singapore. The free-living gametophyte form sold in the aquarium trade has no documented wild population — all hobby specimens are clones of plants distributed by Christel Kasselmann starting in 2001.

decorPreferences: tied to hardscape with fishing line or thread until self-attached

goodWith: fry of egg-scattering and livebearer species

summary: A free-living fern gametophyte (closely allied to Lomariopsis lineata) that forms distinctive lettuce-like, profusely branched, one-cell-thick thalli. Sold as Süsswassertang / Subwassertang — German for 'freshwater seaweed' — and prized in shrimp tanks and fry refuges; easy to keep but distinct from the unrelated liverwort Monosolenium tenerum (sometimes mislabeled with the same name).
careNotes: Subwassertang (Süsswassertang, 'freshwater seaweed') is a botanical curiosity: it is the free-living gametophyte stage of a tropical fern most closely related to Lomariopsis lineata, and it was long mistaken for a thallose liverwort. Christel Kasselmann encountered the plant in her aquarium in 2001 and distributed it through the hobby; a 2009 molecular study by Li et al. resolved it as a fern gametophyte but did not definitively rule out distinct species status. All cultivated stock is believed to descend as clones from that original 2001 introduction, and efforts to induce sporophyte formation in aquaria have so far failed. Morphologically the plant is a flat, profusely branched, lettuce-like thallus only one cell-layer thick, dark green, and 2–10 cm across. It tolerates a wide range of water chemistry — pH 6.0–8.0, soft to moderately hard water, and temperatures 20–28 °C — but dislikes rapid parameter swings, which cause melt. Lighting is best at low to medium intensity (it sulks under intense light); CO2 is not required but accelerates growth meaningfully once the plant is established. Growth is slow at first, then picks up. The plant functions as a free-floater that will self-attach to driftwood and porous stone with time; aquascapers typically tie clumps onto hardscape with fishing line, cotton thread, or hairnet until anchored (several weeks). It is especially valued in shrimp tanks, fry refuges, and dim shaded zones because the dense branched thalli trap biofilm and microfauna that fry and shrimplets graze on. Important taxonomic / labeling note: 'Susswassertang' is sometimes (incorrectly) applied to Monosolenium tenerum (fw-plant-153), which is an unrelated thallose liverwort; the two can look superficially similar but Monosolenium is darker, thicker, and grows in upright cushions, whereas true Subwassertang forms flat lettuce-like sheets that drape and float.
breedingNotes: Propagated exclusively by vegetative fragmentation: pinch, cut, or tear the thallus into smaller pieces and re-tie them to hardscape, or simply let loose pieces drift and reattach on their own. No sexual reproduction (no sporophyte, no spores) has ever been documented in aquarium culture — the population is essentially a clonal lineage descended from the 2001 specimens distributed by Christel Kasselmann.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Süsswassertang
primary.notes: Primary source for identity (fern gametophyte, not liverwort), taxonomy (Lomariopsidaceae, Polypodiales), 2001 introduction by Christel Kasselmann, 2009 molecular study by Li et al. confirming affinity to Lomariopsis lineata, Southeast Asian native range of the sporophyte (humid forests to 1,200 m, epiphytic or on rocks in dry river beds), thalloid one-cell-thick morphology, fragmentation-only propagation, and the confusion with Pellia / Monosolenium.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Subwassertang care guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked light (low to medium), CO2 (not necessary but recommended for optimal growth), pH (6–8), care level (easy/beginner), growth rate (slow initially, accelerates once established), free-floating behavior with self-attachment to driftwood and stone, propagation by cutting/ripping, value in shrimp tanks and fry refuges, and warning that rapid parameter fluctuations cause deterioration.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Lomariopsis
additional.notes: Used for genus context and confirmation that the aquarium plant is an economically important gametophyte form introduced in 2001, closely allied to L. lineata but not formally described as a distinct species. Confirms family Lomariopsidaceae, order Polypodiales.

propagation: tie clumps to hardscape until self-attached
